frontmatter.title: Two statements of the Phoa principle

mainmatter.p: Let \mathbb {I} be an interval object with distinct endpoints 0,1; write \mathopen {} \left [ - \right ] \mathclose {} \colon \mathbb {I} \hookrightarrow \mathsf {Prop} for the embedding sending x: \mathbb {I} to \mathopen {} \left ( x=1 \right ) \mathclose {}. We then inherit a partial order \leq on \mathbb {I} from the implication order along \mathopen {} \left [ - \right ] \mathclose {}. We will write \mathbb {I} _2 for the subset type \mathopen {} \left \{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \right ) \mathclose {} : \mathbb {I} \times \mathbb {I} \, \middle \vert \, x \leq y \right \} \mathclose {}.
Further assume that \leq is closed under disjunction and conjunction in \mathsf {Prop}. Then the following are equivalent:

ol.li: For all \phi \colon \mathbb {I} \to \mathbb {I}, we have \phi = \lambda x \mathpunct {.} \phi \mathopen {} \left ( 0 \right ) \mathclose {} \lor \mathopen {} \left ( x \land \phi \mathopen {} \left ( 1 \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {}.

The evaluation map \mathopen {} \left ( 0,1 \right ) \mathclose {} ^* \colon \mathbb {I} ^ \mathbb {I} \to \mathbb {I} \times \mathbb {I} factors through the inclusion \iota _2 \colon \mathbb {I} _2 \hookrightarrow \mathbb {I} \times \mathbb {I} along an isomorphism H \colon \mathbb {I} ^ \mathbb {I} \to \mathbb {I} _2.

mainmatter.p: First we check that for each \phi \colon \mathbb {I} ^ \mathbb {I}, we have \phi \mathopen {} \left ( 0 \right ) \mathclose {} \leq \phi \mathopen {} \left ( 1 \right ) \mathclose {}. We calculate using our assumption:

Therefore, \mathopen {} \left ( 0,1 \right ) \mathclose {} ^* \colon \mathbb {I} ^ \mathbb {I} \to \mathbb {I} \times \mathbb {I} factors through \iota _2 \colon \mathbb {I} _2 \hookrightarrow \mathbb {I} \times \mathbb {I} along some map H \colon \mathbb {I} ^ \mathbb {I} \to \mathbb {I} _2. We will show that H is an isomorphism by explicitly defining an inverse H ^{-1} \colon \mathbb {I} _2 \to \mathbb {I} ^ \mathbb {I}:
mainmatter.tex: H ^{-1} \mathopen {} \left ( x \leq y \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv \lambda z \mathpunct {.} x \lor \mathopen {} \left ( z \land y \right ) \mathclose {}
mainmatter.p: To check that H \circ H ^{-1} = 1_{ \mathbb {I} _2 }, it suffices to show that \iota _2 \circ H \circ H ^{-1} = \iota _2.
mainmatter.tex: \begin {aligned} \iota _2 \mathopen {} \left ( H \mathopen {} \left ( H ^{-1} \mathopen {} \left ( x \leq y \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} &= \mathopen {} \left ( 0,1 \right ) \mathclose {} ^* \mathopen {} \left ( H ^{-1} \mathopen {} \left ( x \leq y \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} \\ &= \mathopen {} \left ( 0,1 \right ) \mathclose {} ^* \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda z \mathpunct {.} x \lor \mathopen {} \left ( z \land y \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} \\ &= \mathopen {} \left ( x \lor \mathopen {} \left ( 0 \land y \right ) \mathclose {} , x \lor \mathopen {} \left ( 1 \land y \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} \\ &= \mathopen {} \left ( x, x \lor y \right ) \mathclose {} \\ &= \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \right ) \mathclose {} \\ &= \iota _2 \mathopen {} \left ( x \leq y \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}
mainmatter.p: To check that H ^{-1} \circ H = 1_{ \mathbb {I} ^ \mathbb {I} }, we fix \phi : \mathbb {I} ^ \mathbb {I} and compute using our assumption:

Assuming the described factorisation property, we fix \phi \colon \mathbb {I} \to \mathbb {I} to check that \phi = \lambda x \mathpunct {.} \phi \mathopen {} \left ( 0 \right ) \mathclose {} \lor \mathopen {} \left ( x \land \phi \mathopen {} \left ( 1 \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {}. Because H \colon \mathbb {I} ^ \mathbb {I} \to \mathbb {I} _2 is an isomorphism (and therefore an embedding), it follows that \mathopen {} \left ( 0,1 \right ) \mathclose {} ^* \colon \mathbb {I} ^ \mathbb {I} \to \mathbb {I} \times \mathbb {I} is an embedding. Therefore it suffices to show that \mathopen {} \left ( 0,1 \right ) \mathclose {} ^* \phi = \mathopen {} \left ( 0,1 \right ) \mathclose {} ^* \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} \phi \mathopen {} \left ( 0 \right ) \mathclose {} \lor \mathopen {} \left ( x \land \phi \mathopen {} \left ( 1 \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {}.
Above we have used the fact that \phi \mathopen {} \left ( 0 \right ) \mathclose {} \leq \phi \mathopen {} \left ( 1 \right ) \mathclose {}, which follows from the factorisation property.
